There's many more samples of contracts and marketing documents and those sorts of things in there. And we're going to plow through all of those tomorrow. Another thing I want to look at now is... If you go to... I'm going to do next. And I get this out of the way. Jump to section 7, which is resources. Everybody always asks me what books they ought to read. Here's the list. So you will find... There are no page numbers. Just find your tab divider number 7 and you're there. So the books that I think are sort of essential. And, you know, if you've read them, it wouldn't hurt not to reread them in the context of what we're talking about here in the two days. But certainly, Ringer's book, the original, winning through intimidation, not the sort of neutered, newer version of it. Many of you here have read that book. It would be easier to ask how many you had. How many have? The old. The old? Really? Okay. And some of you have read the new. Okay. I'd go read the old anyway, if I were you. There's significant differences of between the old and the new. And a lot of people, of course, were turned off by the title. But now in the context of our conversation, you shouldn't be turned off by the title at all. And really the most useful stuff in the book is the early stuff. You know, how he behaved when he had no reputation and nobody knew who he was. How many have read Michael Korda's book, Power? Good. Stephen, you're showing your age. That book, as I said, may be hard to find. I'm not sure. But I suggest you make an effort. Michael Korda at one time was probably the most powerful guy in the publishing business. And was president, I believe, over to chairman of the board of Random House at the time that he wrote the book. Simon Schuster? Yeah, okay. Well, you probably know him, don't you? Yeah. What's he doing now? I don't know. He had cancer. I don't know what happened with all of that. But anyway, he wrote the book Power while he was in charge of Simon Schuster, so like he didn't need an agent to get published, which was kind of nice. But it's really a very good book. The sequel to it called Success is not so good. But Power was a very good book. It might not be easy to find, but it's worth trying to find. Thick-faced black heart. How many? Very good. But a bunch of you don't have your hands up. I would suggest that book. And if you haven't read Psycho-Cyber-Cyber-Dedicts, read them back to back. Read Psycho-Cyber-Dedicts and then read Thick-Face Blackheart, because one really sort of picks up where the other one leaves off. Green's book, The 48 Laws of Power, how many? Oh, not that many. Now we found one. Hardly anybody's got. A little heavy lifting. It's not like the most fun book to read, but it is well worth reading. And so I suggest you get your hands on that. Again, in the context of things we've been talking about, you'll get it. You'll like it. I quoted it a number of times today and will tomorrow. Not on this list is Neil Strauss' book, The Game. How many of you have read Neil's book? Yeah, okay. Well, the rest of you. It's a good book to read. It's a fun book to read. And if you view it as a sales training book, rather than what it is written as, well, I mean, for those of you who don't know, Neil, there's a whole industry and subculture of how to meet women teachers and experts and gurus practitioners and it's a whole world unto itself. You might know, been a member of ours for years, came to a copywriting seminar a month years ago, Ross Jefferies, who's like kind of the evil one of the tribe. His thing is speed seduction. Well, this is an adult group. I'll tell you something very funny about Ross Jefferies. I'm doing this real small copywriting seminar.
